title: Heaps
description: JavaScript has no built-in heap, so you need to know how to implement a min-heap manually or use sorted arrays for top-K problems. Understanding the heap concept is critical for priority queue interview questions.

sections.title: No built-in heap in JavaScript
sections.explanation: Unlike Python or Java, JavaScript has no built-in priority queue or heap. In interviews, you have three options: implement a MinHeap class, use a sorted array for small inputs, or explain the heap approach and implement with sort. Know this gap and be ready to discuss the trade-offs.

exercise.prompt: Given `nums = [7, 2, 9, 1, 5, 3]`, find the 3rd smallest element using sort. Print the sorted array joined by commas, then print the 3rd smallest value.

validation.message: Use numeric sort with (a, b) => a - b comparator

exercise.hint: Sort ascending with a - b. The kth smallest is at index k-1, so nums[3-1] = nums[2].

sections.title: Manual min-heap implementation
sections.explanation: A min-heap is a complete binary tree where each parent is smaller than its children. Store it in an array where node i has children at 2i+1 and 2i+2, and parent at Math.floor((i-1)/2). push() adds to the end and bubbles up; pop() swaps root with last, removes last, and bubbles down.

exercise.prompt: Complete the MinHeap with _bubbleUp. Push values 5, 3, 7, 1 into the heap, then pop twice and print each popped value. The smallest values should come out first.

validation.message: Implement a MinHeap class with push and pop methods

exercise.hint: In bubbleUp, swap with parent when current value < parent value.

sections.title: Top-K with sort
sections.explanation: For top-K problems when you don't need a full heap implementation, sort the array and take the first K or last K elements. This is O(n log n) vs O(n log k) with a real heap, but it's clean and fast to write in interviews. Always mention the trade-off.

exercise.prompt: Find the top 3 largest values from [8, 2, 15, 3, 11, 7] using sort and slice. Print them joined by commas.

validation.message: Use sort descending then slice to get top-K elements

exercise.hint: Sort descending with b - a, then slice(0, 3) for the top 3.

sections.title: Priority queue concept
sections.explanation: A priority queue always serves the highest-priority element first. In interview problems like merge K sorted lists, Dijkstra's algorithm, or task scheduling, explain the priority queue concept even when using a sort-based approach. Interviewers want to hear you reason about O(log n) insert/extract vs O(n log n) re-sort.

exercise.prompt: Simulate a priority queue: push values 5, 1, 3, 2, 4 into an array, sort ascending, and process items from lowest to highest using shift(). Print each processed value on its own line.

validation.message: Use sort for ordering and shift to extract the highest-priority element

exercise.hint: Sort ascending with a - b, then shift() removes the smallest (highest priority) each time.
